statement.markdown: Solve the problem and fix the bugs.
statement.inputFormat: The graph will be represented as an adjacency list, where each index represents a vertex and its value is a list of its neighbors.
statement.outputFormat: Return true if the graph contains a cycle, false otherwise.
statement.notes: This graph can contain self-loops and multiple edges between the same pair of vertices.

hints.text: Use DFS traversal to detect cycle in the graph.

editorial.title: Detecting Cycle in Undirected Graph
editorial.intuition: We can use the concept of DFS to detect cycle in the graph. If a node is already visited, we can check if it is part of the current DFS traversal path.
editorial.bruteForce: We can use a brute force approach by checking all possible paths in the graph. However, this approach has a high time complexity of O(n^2).
editorial.optimalSolution: We can use a recursive DFS function to detect cycle in the graph. If a node is already visited and it is part of the current DFS traversal path, we can return true indicating the presence of a cycle.
editorial.proofOfCorrectness: We can prove the correctness of the optimal solution by showing that if the graph contains a cycle, the DFS traversal will visit the same node multiple times, indicating the presence of a cycle.
editorial.dryRun: Let's consider a sample graph: 0 -> 1 -> 2 -> 0. If we start the DFS traversal from node 0, we will visit nodes 1 and 2 and then back to node 0, indicating the presence of a cycle.
commonMistakes: Not using a visited set to keep track of visited nodes.
Not checking if a node is part of the current DFS traversal path.
